stem: Consider the fixed-point iteration \( x_{n+1} = g(x_n) \) with \( g(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The point \( x^{*} = [[p]] \) is a fixed point (so \( g(x^{*}) = x^{*} \)). Local convergence is governed by the value \( |g'(x^{*})| \). Compute \( |g'(x^{*})| \).
solution: Here \( g'(x) = 2([[a]])x + ([[b]]) \), so \( g'(x^{*}) = 2([[a]])([[p]]) + ([[b]]) \) and \( |g'(x^{*})| = [[answer]] \). Local convergence requires this value to be less than \( 1 \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the absolute value and reports the signed derivative \( g'(x^{*}) \).
This uses the wrong derivative \( a x + b \), dropping the factor of 2 on the \( x^{2} \) term.
This evaluates \( g(x^{*}) = x^{*} \) (the fixed point itself) rather than \( g'(x^{*}) \).
This drops the constant term \( b \) of \( g'(x^{*}) \).
This uses the wrong sign on \( b \) inside \( g'(x^{*}) \).
This is off by one from \( |g'(x^{*})| \).

stem: Consider the fixed-point iteration \( x_{n+1} = g(x_n) \) with \( g(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{x + [[b]]} \). The point \( x^{*} = [[p]] \) is a fixed point of \( g \) (so \( g(x^{*}) = x^{*} \)). Local convergence is governed by \( |g'(x^{*})| \). Compute \( |g'(x^{*})| \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the absolute value and reports the signed derivative \( g'(x^{*}) \).
This forgets to square the denominator from the quotient rule.
This uses \( b \) alone instead of \( x^{*}+b \) in the denominator.
This multiplies by the squared denominator instead of dividing by it.
This is one more than \( |g'(x^{*})| \).
This is one less than \( |g'(x^{*})| \).

stem: Consider the fixed-point iteration \( x_{n+1} = g(x_n) \) with \( g(x) = \sqrt{[[a]]x + [[b]]} \). The point \( x^{*} = [[p]] \) is a positive fixed point of \( g \) (so \( g(x^{*}) = x^{*} \)). Local convergence is governed by \( |g'(x^{*})| \). Compute \( |g'(x^{*})| \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the absolute value and reports the signed derivative \( g'(x^{*}) \).
This forgets the factor of 2 from differentiating the square root (chain rule).
This multiplies by \( 2x^{*} \) instead of dividing by it.
This squares the fixed point in the denominator instead of using it linearly.
This is one more than \( |g'(x^{*})| \).
This is one less than \( |g'(x^{*})| \).

stem: Consider the fixed-point iteration \( x_{n+1} = g(x_n) \) with \( g(x) = [[A]]x^{2} + ([[B]])x \) on the interval \( [[[m]], [[M]]] \). What is the contraction constant \( K = \max_{x \in [[[m]], [[M]]]} |g'(x)| \) on this interval, which bounds the convergence rate of the iteration there?
solution: Since \( g'(x) = 2([[A]])x + ([[B]]) \) is linear (hence monotonic), its maximum absolute value on \( [[[m]], [[M]]] \) occurs at an endpoint: \( K = \max(|g'([[m]])|, |g'([[M]])|) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only checks the left endpoint, ignoring that the maximum could be at the right endpoint.
This only checks the right endpoint, ignoring that the maximum could be at the left endpoint.
This takes the minimum of the endpoint values instead of the maximum.
This evaluates \( g' \) at the midpoint of the interval instead of checking both endpoints.
This is one more than the correct contraction constant.
This is one less than the correct contraction constant.

stem: A fixed-point iteration has contraction constant \( K = \dfrac{1}{[[r]]} \), so its error satisfies \( |x_n - x^{*}| \le K^{n}|x_0 - x^{*}| \). If the initial error is \( |x_0 - x^{*}| = [[e0]] \), what is the least number of iterations \( n \) that guarantees the error is at most \( [[tol]] \)?
solution: We need \( K^{n}|x_0-x^{*}| \le [[tol]] \), i.e. \( \left(\dfrac{1}{[[r]]}\right)^{n}[[e0]] \le [[tol]] \), i.e. \( [[r]]^{n} \ge \dfrac{[[e0]]}{[[tol]]} \). So \( n = \left\lceil \log_{[[r]]}\!\left(\dfrac{[[e0]]}{[[tol]]}\right) \right\rceil = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This rounds down instead of up.
This inverts the ratio inside the logarithm.
This uses base-10 logarithms instead of base \( r \).
This overshoots by one iteration.
This is one iteration short of the guarantee.
This overshoots the required number of iterations by two.
